one_line_takeaway: Transforming untrusted LLM input to signal its provenance — via datamarking or (for capable models) encoding — cuts indirect-prompt-injection success from over 50% to under 2% with little task-quality loss.
key_finding: Spotlighting reduces attack success rate from greater than 50% to below 2% in the authors' experiments with minimal impact on task efficacy. Concretely: plain system-prompt instructions have only a modest effect (almost no benefit for GPT-3.5-Turbo). Delimiting roughly halves ASR (GPT-3.5-Turbo baseline ~60% cut by about half) but is easily subverted if the attacker knows the delimiters. Datamarking is stronger: in summarization it cuts GPT-3.5-Turbo ASR from ~50% to 3.10% and text-davinci-003 from ~40% to 0.00%; in document Q&A it reaches 8.0% (GPT-3.5-Turbo), 1.0% (GPT-4), and 0.00% (text-davinci-003). Encoding performs best, bringing ASR to 0.0% in summarization and 1.8% in Q&A on GPT-3.5-Turbo. On the four NLP benchmarks, datamarking shows no detrimental impact on task performance, but encoding only preserves performance on high-capacity models: GPT-4 handles encoded input well while GPT-3.5-Turbo suffers (decoding errors/hallucinations), so encoding is recommended only for the most capable models. The appendix few-shot defense drives GPT-3.5-Turbo ASR below 5% but the authors caution it may overfit to known attack tactics and risks label leakage, so they prefer the structural spotlighting approach.
core_contribution: The paper introduces "spotlighting," a family of prompt-engineering defenses against indirect prompt injection (XPIA) attacks on LLMs. The core insight is that the LLM cannot distinguish trusted system instructions ("code") from untrusted external input ("data") because it operates on a boundary-less token stream; spotlighting applies a transformation to the untrusted input that gives it a reliable, continuous signal of provenance so the model can tell input text apart from instructions. The authors describe three concrete instantiations, ordered by increasing strength: (1) delimiting — wrapping input in special begin/end tokens the model is told to distrust; (2) datamarking — interleaving a special token (e.g. '^') throughout the input, replacing whitespace, so the whole untrusted block carries the marker; and (3) encoding — transforming the input with a well-known algorithm (base64, ROT13, binary) that capable LLMs can implicitly decode. They build a synthetic evaluation corpus, define a strict Attack Success Rate (ASR) metric, and empirically show spotlighting cuts ASR sharply with little cost to task quality. The delimiting idea and an early version of the approach are credited to prior work (Yi et al., ref [2], arXiv:2312.14197), which this paper explicitly extends; datamarking and encoding are the paper's own contributions.
threat_model: Indirect prompt injection (XPIA): an LLM application processes external/untrusted data (websites, emails, text messages, documents) concatenated with trusted user commands into one boundary-less prompt. An attacker embeds adversarial instructions inside that external data; because the LLM cannot distinguish code (system instructions) from data (untrusted text), it may follow the injected instructions as if they were the user's, hijacking the innocent user's session and running under their credentials (potentially triggering downstream actions like data exfiltration via plugins/skills). The paper distinguishes XPIA from direct user prompt injection/jailbreaking (UPIA) and treats XPIA as a superset. For the defenses, the authors adopt a strong adversary assumption: the entire system prompt may be leaked, so a static delimiter or a bidirectional cipher (e.g. ROT13, which an attacker can pre-encode to invert) is exploitable; they therefore favor dynamic/randomized marking tokens and one-way transforms (base64) the attacker cannot control.
limitations: The authors state they lack a clear mechanistic understanding of WHY spotlighting works (they offer only an analogy to in-band vs. out-of-band telecom signaling as intuition). The evaluation uses a synthetic corpus of simple keyword-payload attacks rather than diverse or naturalistic real-world attacks, and ASR is acknowledged to be task-dependent and hard to interpret absolutely (they emphasize relative trends). Encoding degrades task performance on weaker models and is only safe on high-capacity LLMs like GPT-4, requiring per-use-case validation. Basic datamarking via whitespace can be evaded by attack strings containing no spaces (mitigated by proposed but not fully evaluated dynamic tokenizer-based marking). The few-shot defense is explicitly cautioned as prone to overfitting current attack knowledge and label leakage. Models are limited to the GPT family (2023 versions); no open-source or non-GPT models are tested. The authors also concede spotlighting creates separation but is "not perfectly secure against interference," and a true multi-channel out-of-band solution is left as future work.

datasets_used: Synthetic corpus of 1000 documents containing keyword-payload prompt-injection attacks (authors' own)
quantitative_results: Abstract-level headline: spotlighting reduces ASR from >50% to below 2% with minimal task impact
Delimiting: GPT-3.5-Turbo baseline ~60% reduced by about half
Plain system-prompt instructions: only modest ASR reduction (almost none for GPT-3.5-Turbo)
Encoding preserves NLP task performance on GPT-4 but degrades it on GPT-3.5-Turbo; datamarking shows no detrimental effect on any of the four benchmarks
Appendix few-shot defense: GPT-3.5-Turbo ASR reduced to below 5% (reported with caveats)
Experiments run at temperature 1.0 with no notable temperature effect on susceptibility
baselines_compared: Prior work Yi et al. [2] (arXiv:2312.14197), which this paper extends
